{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        layers: list[nn.Module] = [nn.Flatten(), nn.LayerNorm(input_dim)]","old":"        layers: list[nn.Module] = [nn.Flatten()]","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:
                layers.append(nn.LayerNorm(hidden_dim))
            layers.append(nn.GELU())","old":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:
                layers.append(nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim))
            layers.append(nn.GELU())","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current model is still very small and uses BatchNorm in a fixed-step, small-budget regime where running-stat mismatch can hurt validation. A modest width increase plus LayerNorm on the flattened input and hidden activations should give a better-conditioned MLP without adding much complexity or optimizer risk.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        layers: list[nn.Module] = [nn.Flatten()]","old":"        layers: list[nn.Module] = [nn.Flatten(), nn.LayerNorm(input_dim)]","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:
                layers.append(nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim))
            layers.append(nn.GELU())","old":"            if USE_BATCHNORM:
                layers.append(nn.LayerNorm(hidden_dim))
            layers.append(nn.GELU())","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The previous LayerNorm and width increase clearly hurt, so this candidate restores the better-conditioned earlier MLP and then reallocates the fixed 256-step budget toward more data coverage per step. A moderately larger batch with a modest LR increase is a simple way to exploit the fixed-step evaluator without adding architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gain so far came from using the fixed 256-step budget to process more examples. Pushing batch size further should increase data coverage to roughly a full epoch within budget, and a proportional LR increase keeps the optimizer step scale aligned with the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current schedule and batch allocation are already productive, so the next clean lever is optimizer dynamics. AdamW with slightly stronger decoupled weight decay often improves short-budget generalization for MLPs more reliably than Adam with L2-style decay.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimizer, batch size, and scheduler now look reasonably tuned for the fixed-step budget, so the next clean test is whether the current MLP is simply capacity-limited. The earlier regression mixed width and normalization changes together; this isolates a modest width increase while keeping the better BatchNorm-based layout intact.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The isolated width increase helped, which suggests the model still benefits from extra capacity. Adding one more hidden block is the next simple architecture test and should improve representational power while preserving the same optimizer and schedule that are already working.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Capacity increases have continued to help, which raises the risk of short-horizon overfitting or brittle activations in the deeper MLP. A small dropout rate is a simple regularization test that can preserve the stronger architecture while nudging validation loss down.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The dropout change was clearly harmful in this short-budget regime, which is consistent with earlier results favoring pure capacity and faster fitting. Reverting that regularization is the highest-confidence next step before testing more subtle refinements.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger, deeper MLP is still improving under the current schedule, which suggests it may benefit from a slightly more aggressive peak step size. A modest LR increase is the simplest way to test whether the current configuration is still under-driving optimization within the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"With only 256 optimizer steps, AdamW may benefit from a shorter second-moment timescale than the default 0.999 setting. Lowering beta2 should let the optimizer adapt faster to the current deeper, larger-batch regime without changing the overall schedule or architecture.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Earlier improvements consistently came from using the fixed 256-step budget to cover more training examples, and the current optimizer is already adapted to faster short-horizon dynamics. A further batch-size increase is the cleanest remaining test of whether validation loss is still data-coverage limited at this budget.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The largest improvement so far came from increasing batch size again, which indicates this fixed-step workload remains strongly data-coverage limited. Pushing to 512 is the simplest next test to see whether even more examples per optimizer step continue lowering validation loss before optimization quality starts to degrade.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last two largest gains came from increasing batch size again, which strongly suggests this verifier remains dominated by training-example coverage rather than per-step noise benefits. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The recent gains have been dominated by larger batch sizes, and the effect has remained strong even as wall time increased. That pattern still points to sample coverage being the main limiter under the fixed 256-step budget, so moving to 1024 is the most direct continuation of the best trend.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After several successful batch-size increases, the current configuration is likely running with a conservative step size relative to its much larger effective batch. {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The higher learning rate regressed, so the larger-batch regime still appears to prefer the previous 2.5e-3 setting. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1536","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size scaling has been the dominant source of improvement throughout this run, and the current best point still came from the largest tested batch. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1536","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Across the run, larger batch sizes have been the single most reliable and highest-yield direction, with the latest increase still producing a large gain.
